sources.short: Find a Grave — Old Eastbury Cemetery
Find a Grave — Old Eastbury Cemetery

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at thirty-nine with eight children
narrative_blocks.body: Esther was thirty-nine years old when her husband John House IV died in November 1798, leaving her with eight children — the youngest Cyprian still an infant of about eighteen months. She never remarried and lived another fifty years, dying in East Hartford in 1848 at age eighty-nine.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his father John House IV — eldest son carrying the patrilineal name forward.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost three young children including two in 1817
narrative_blocks.body: Betsey lost three of her six children before they reached age five — Walter and Orange S. died together in 1817 (ages four and three, suggesting an epidemic or shared illness), and her infant Sylvester died in 1829. Of her three surviving children — Roxanna, Sidney, and Edwin — all reached old age.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin Morgan House
narrative_blocks.body: Roxy married her first cousin Morgan House in 1842 — both were grandchildren of Esther Hooker House. Roxy descended through Esther's daughter Betsey Hills; Morgan through Esther's son John House Jr. Their families had migrated together from Glastonbury Connecticut to Houseville Lewis County New York in the early nineteenth century.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his father Orange Hills.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost at seven between siblings' births
narrative_blocks.body: Roxana was Esther's fourth child, born 1787 between Clarissa (1786) and Sophronia (1788). She died in 1794 at age seven — four years before her father's death and three years before her youngest brother Cyprian was born. She is the only documented child Esther lost in childhood.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost his father as an infant of one
narrative_blocks.body: Cyprian was Esther's eighth and youngest child, born April 5, 1797 in Glastonbury Connecticut. His father John House IV died on November 14, 1798 when Cyprian was only nineteen months old. Cyprian later migrated north to Turin Lewis County New York, married Sophia Risley in 1819, raised four children there, and lived to age eighty-six.

narrative_blocks.header: New Jersey-born wife joined Cyprian's Turin migration
narrative_blocks.body: Sophia was born in Gloucester County New Jersey and married Cyprian House in 1819 — soon after his arrival in Turin Lewis County NY. They were married fifty-four years and raised four children in Turin together. Sophia died there in June 1873, ten years before Cyprian.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Risley' carries his mother Sophia Risley House's maiden surname forward.

career.title: Farmer
career.notes: Farmed his entire life in Turin Lewis County NY — described in obituary as 'an honored citizen and farmer.'

narrative_blocks.header: Lifelong Turin farmer died where he was born
narrative_blocks.body: Chester was born in Turin and lived there all seventy-one years of his life, dying on the same spot where he had been born. His obituary called him 'a man of strict integrity, an honored citizen and farmer' who would be greatly missed in the community. He was survived by his wife Olive Mickel and two of their children.
Eliza died in 1844, aged about fifteen.

burialCemetery.name: Old Eastbury Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Glastonbury CT cemetery. Burial site of Col. Samuel Talcott (T01001, 1732-1780), whose Revolutionary War-era gravestone survives intact. His first wife Mary Smith Talcott (U00042, d.1770) is unconfirmed but per family tradition likely buried here too, having predeceased him by ten years. Resting place of John House IV (X01233, 1758-1798, age 39-40) — husband of Esther Hooker House. He died in Hartford in November 1798 leaving Esther a widow at thirty-nine with seven living children (and Cyprian an infant of about eighteen months). She lived another fifty years and died in 1848 at age eighty-nine. Esther herself is buried at Old Eastbury per FG memorial — though her primary residence shifted to East Hartford after John IV's death. [Merged from duplicate CEM246, Stage 6Y-652.]
